commentBody: Yay! That was a Friday puzzle like olden days. Over and over I thought I was not gonna get it. The northwest and the southeast fell, then it filled in through the middle. Then the southwest then the northeast. Thank you, thank you, thank you! Just under 26 minutes and I’m ecstatic.
Tolstoy would have used KAtarINA too if he had thought of it, probably.

Some good clues made this one fun.  

Low volume [TEASPOON], Game [UNAFRAID], It has a light bulb [DAFFODIL], Peace sign [DONOTDISTURB], Acrobat’s display [PDFFILES]. Those and others made for a breezy themeless.

Wow, that was one of my slowest Fridays in recent memory.

I think this was probably one of the tougher Fridays recently ... and I am 100% in favor of more and more puzzles like this.

I'm too punchdrunk from being buffeted hither and thither by this puzzle to be specific, but I enjoyed a whole boatload of super-clever clues that kept me guessing for a long time. A very fine Friday experience for me.

Well, time for bed. I wanted to see more comments. This is getting better and better because it is clear that the puzzlesphere is really struggling with this one. Fantastic. Mr. Spooner can submit a puzzle anytime he wants.

A little bit before the puzzle dropped at 9 PM CDT, I was talking with two friends, one of whom is a woman who really enjoys anything she can do in the water. I’ve known her for almost 40 years, and I realized that she was a NAIAD… a water nymph. Of course, an hour or so later, there is DRYADS in this puzzle. As Jack McCoy said: sometimes, life is a funny old dog.

Loved it. Felt a lot like pre 2020 Friday/Saturday puzzles. I felt like I was going to be stuck forever in multiple places and suddenly everything just seemed to fall into place (which still doesn't happen with pre 2020 puzzles for me sadly). More such Friday puzzles please.

Excellent. Friday-level difficulty (for me), and full of great clues. I solved it before I saw the title -- and that's just the one I wanted to mention: "Acrobat's display" had me stuck for a while, until I realized with a groan -- PDF FILE. Hah!
My only quibble is that I think of Brian Eno as composing ethereal, mellow, experimental "music for airports," but maybe I'm just not familiar with his rockier output.

I enjoyed Sean McGowan's column, very interesting to learn about these "cheater squares".
I don't usually read the column because I find that often it's just written from the POV of a regular solver: friendly and sympathetic, but ultimately not very illuminating. I'd much rather some expert or technical insights about things like grid design and other aspects of construction that I don't even know exist.
I wouldn't have appreciated today's marvellously clean grid - quite a beauty in its elegant simplicity, even just aesthetically  - if I hadn't read the column.
Thanks Andrew and Sean!

Trying again, emus:

This has been one of the best solving weeks, starting with Sunday, that I can remember, and today’s offering continues the trend.
I can hardly forget Mr. Spooner’s first offering, which was New Year’s Day, since he happens to have the same exact name as my crossword loving boss whom I mailed to ask how he could have submitted a puzzle without telling me. I was impressed then and even more impressed today. This was a proper Friday!
The only gimmes were trivia that I happened to know—BOGART, VINNIE Barbarino, Anna KARENINA, and THEOLOGY as a stab—and without them I’m not sure what other footholds I would have had—and even with them had to work hard in every crack and crevice. The SE almost did me in since I had HINNY before JENNY (I guess I shouldn’t pull answers out of my α$$ and I couldn’t find a preposition after SNEAK that looked  like it would go with an N and D going down (oh, that Acrobat!)
Too many sparkling clues to mention and few duds, but I’d call out IN-LAWS, DAFFODILS and DONOTDISTURB for best clues of the week.
Nice reminder of yesterday’s puzzle with Secondhand Offerings. Please no pearl clutchers for W*F, it’s not a debut and civilization will not collapse. Finally, ESOPHAGI has the same number of letters as RIGATONI for tubes going down, but luckily I was so sure about BOGART I didn’t swallow it for too long.
Thanks Andrew and editors!!!
I was stuck forever on the southeast corner, trying to shoe-horn in "PE Skills" instead of "PDF Files," then finally made the context switch.  Nice one!

Here to say that, despite the comments so far, I actually thought this was one of the easiest Fridays in recent weeks and I was seconds off from my best time.

This is why I think we should pay less attention to the complaints in this forum about declining difficulty because there will always be different strokes for different folks… what is easy & enjoyable for you may not be (&& probably won’t be) easy & enjoyable for me.

Great debut, Andrew!

So, regarding 46A. I personally have no problem with it. But I'm puzzled about where the line is drawn between acceptable for the NYT and beyond the pale. In this case, the phrase spelled out in full would be a no-no, but the abbreviation is acceptable? I'm not complaining, only trying to understand what the rules are.

I found this one *HARD*

I thought I might never get anything, but then, finally, words started to fill in here and there and I got the momentum i needed.
good puzzle!

Brutal, I loved it. I had next to nothing after my first pass through the crosses and downs, and only a guess for !?!?!? unlocked the lower left which opened up the puzzle. 

50% over my average time for Friday, but lots of fun.

This felt hard but solved quite quickly, in the end.

The trivia-heavy SE corner involved many guesses but was ultimately doable. 

Some nice misdirection all over the grid.

And since I'm not a pearl-clutcher, I have no complaints. 

Nice.

I could barely do any of yesterdays puzzle (far too many Americanisms) but this one I found very fun! Lots of 'abstract thinking' clues and solutions. Tubes that go down was a highlight for me and blew open the puzzle once I realised.

Had trouble in the centre, particularly with "Coalesces" which originally was "UNITE" and led to many issues.
PDQ

Even though I should know better, I always hope "Rocker Brian" will be MAY. I mean I guess I know well enough to not hopefully fill it in. I always wait for crosses and (so far) I'm always disappointed when the constructor is once again not a Queen fan.

Had nothing filled for the first five minutes before I seeded answers with a ton of esses; filled northwest corner quickly, and the rest came naturally in the next 10 minutes. Well designed and actually felt like a Friday/Saturday grid.

I enjoyed the 'Games' 27A and 34D and Sports World 7A, 19A, 20A, 22A , 1D, 6D, 10D, and 47D (I'm sure they must have a team in some sport!).
And come on, a guy named Spooner gives us a clue to TEASPOON. How cute is that!
Good legit Friday puzzle. More like this please

The main question I have is this:

What's so important about STILTON cheese that we saved a fairly ordinary quote from George Orwell about it?

Or is there some joke that I'm missing?
Awful puzzle, too many clues that I thought were pretty far-reaching once revealed.  Not a fun one at all for me.

Eating the final plate of shredded leafy greens would be the last slaw.

(But maybe you could try to cabbage patch things up.)

Great Friday. Great misdirects. 
Seemed like a Friday from OLDEN days. Plus our boy, Brian ENO, made an appearance. 

But this is not your father’s NYT. 
46A. LOL. I entered it, but was sure I would have to erase. 

I miss these kind of late week puzzles. After 20 minutes, I’m staring at a bunch of blind guesses. thinking, I’m toast.  10 more minutes, and I’m done.  

Also. I’m gonna have to look this up. 
But do we say DESERT ISLAND, because it’s all sand like a desert?  Or because of deserted, like left behind / uninhabited.  But if the latter, why do we say it like the former.  And why is deserted pronounced like dessert-ed. Man, I was born here, and I don’t get American English half the time. 

Cue the gilligans island theme song.

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
A perfect "these-days" Friday.

Had all the required ingredients of a late-week Eloi grid.

I do miss the Morlock grids, though...

Today’s clueing can only be described as outrageous, in a good way. I am gobsmacked by some of those misdirects. 

PS Yesterday i found out about the etymology of “clue”. It was originally “clew”, which means a ball of thread, after the ball of thread that Ariadne gave to Theseus to help him escape the labyrinth. (Who knew? I hadn’t a clue!)

What’s more, clew derives from a root meaning to ball up or clump together, from which we also get glue, gluteus maximus, and clay!
One of these days, the clue will be asking for a musical Brian and the answer will be May.

I would have to go back to my old lecture notes to be sure, but I'm pretty sure Ivan III was the first TSAR to use that title, and Ivan IV is typically given the sobriquet "The Terrible." If I'm right, a rare error indeed. Thanks for all the joy of the crosswords!

It’s interesting to see 38D {Dillydally] and 49A DAFFODIL crossing in the same puzzle. I’ve been pulling gazillions of daffodil plants out of my garden this week and the bulbs are sitting on the concrete. It seems that no matter how many bulbs I removed last season, they still come back in droves.

From Wikipedia: "From at least the 16th century, "daffadown dilly" and "daffydowndilly" have appeared as alternative names" for daffodils.

For anyone interested: Jamie Jeopardy almost had another close call last night but managed to maintain his championship. The Final Jeopardy question in the category "The 50s" asked what was deemed safe and effective in 1955, ten years after the death of an important person. Jeremy got the answer right: The Salk Polio Vaccine. The other guy had it wrong: he said "penicillin," which I believe was in use way before the 1950s. Discovered 1928; used in World War II; Nobel Prize, 1945.

By the way, Jamie said in an interview that aired yesterday that he is too busy to study for Jeopardy because of his law school obligations!

I believe @CLM's comment on this constructor's last puzzle -- his NYT debut -- is worth a reprise:

"At last! A puzzle in which *every single entry* is a Spoonerism!"
Now that was a proper Friday. Southeast held me up a bit. It was fun.

After two passes through this, I'm deep in the woods, as in lost.  Thought I had the SE corner set with my A answers, but the D clues did not match up.  Have to resort to look-ups & hope that  something clicks.

I did this one while trying to follow the end of the Mets' heart attack-inducing win last night.  I turned off the TV with the intention of putting it on via the MLB website, which I'm entitled to do, as I pay for the network that carries it on TV.  I couldn't get it to work, and had to settle for mlb.com's play-by-play "ticker."  At the same time, my wife is texting me her own play-by-play from another room.  Needless to say, this was one of the most distracted solves I've done in a long time, and wound up solving 55.2% slower than my Friday average.  Overall, the puzzle seemed much harder than usual, but I chalked it up to all the distractions.

I was gratified, though, to find the following on xwstats.com:

🌎  Global Stats
Difficulty
Very Hard
Median Solve Time
15:56
Median Solver
32% slower
⚡18% of users solved faster than their Friday average.
6% solved much faster (>20%) than their Friday average.
🐢82% of users solved slower than their Friday average.
60% solved much slower (>20%) than their Friday average.

So it wasn't just my circumstances...it was a hard puzzle for most.

This was as delightful as yesterday’s was abominable (in a good, tough way).
I surprised myself at how quickly I whizzed through this, chuckling at the long spans.
I love OXTAIL but didn’t know it was a Caribbean staple. I buy oxtail from my local butcher to turn into lovely, gelatinous stews and soups (with apologies to the vegans out there). 

I knew 42A was RAF, but the PD I already had for 35D made me doubt myself. I held off filling in almost to the end, before the lightbulb went off over my head.

8D made me think of a candle jar one of the boys gave me a while back; ‘Mum’s last nerve. Oh look, it’s on fire’. I  have no idea what they were trying to convey. I have the patience of a saint. As I frequently yell at them, alongside 46A, but in full technicolour. We embrace Olde Anglo-Saxon vernacular in this household.

Oh.  Man.  I loved this.  Wit, humor, grit, and beauty housed in an elegant grid design.  Stellar.  

Let me flesh this out with an example.  The puzzle has six NYT answer debuts, four of which are so lovely – DESERT ISLAND, DO NOT DISTURB, BUSINESSLIKE, and THE LAST STRAW.  So, there’s beauty.  

Three are given excellent wordplay clues – [Where someone might be washed up?], [Peace sign?], and [You just can’t take it].  There’s wit and humor.  

The fourth, BUSINESSLIKE, is clued toughly, with the could-be-a-noun-or-verb [Professional].  There’s grit.

As for skill?  Each of these four answers crosses two of the others.  What?  Really?  Wow!

Andrew, I was over the moon over your last puzzle, a NYT debut, and called your voice promising. Your creation today fulfilled that promise, and more please.  High props and much gratitude for this!
That was difficult! What a Friday should be… it was the bottom right that killed me and I have to admit to a DNF as I had to look a couple up down there 😩 but a proper Friday it was 👏👏

WT ϕ !?!?!?!
[Are we allowed to write "WT ϕ"?]

That was a puzzle!
So many misdirects!  So few gimmes!
More than once I was unsure if I'd be able to finish on my own.  More than once I thought I'd need to rely on Google for help.
More than once I cursed at the puzzle in frustration and confusion.
I mean, "It has a light bulb"?!?  What am I supposed to do with that?  "Game"?  How many meanings does it have?  Which one is being referenced here?

And, in case I haven't been clear, I loved it.
Favorite clue:  "Peace sign?"; followed closely by "Acrobat's display".
Thanks for a proper Friday puzzler, Andrew Spooner!
(Spooner? Huh? Is that how that crazy TEASPOON made its way into the puzzle?)

Loved it!

Personal favorites: BIRTHS, RIGATONI, TEASPOON, PDFFILES, DENIAL, DAFFODILS. None of those were *hard* (and the schtick with food that goes “down” is certainly well-worn), but they all tickled me. And I enjoyed most of the remaining fill enough to overlook ENO.

The most difficult puzzle I've done in a long time, just so hard to get an entry point. Had it not been for 17A, might still be staring at it. Let me renew my request to ban the use of rappers and children's book authors. There's just too many of them.
Great puzzle and not that hard, until the SE corner, which was demonic.

oooEEEE!! That was good! So hard I almost gave up and looked up a few in the SE corner. I did check spelling on KARENINA and DRYAD. When I finally realized it was PDFFILES, I laughed out loud. Such an excellent misdirect, plus the PD start made it seem so unlikely. I tried many things for 42A. RCA? RAF? R ?!?!?! Now we also have an emu-safe abbreviation for 46A (WT_). 

So many great clues, almost no junk. I don't think we see ENO too often anymore. Just my opinion. Plus he was not clued as ambient or whatever they usually use. I had just enough answers I was pretty sure about to keep me going, but I finished in well over average time. 

Thanks, Andrew!

Thought I'd posted earlier but it never showed up. Anyway... Typical tough Friday for me. Just a bunch of really clever misleading clues. Had to look up a couple of things, but mostly just working the crosses and pondering. Ended up being an enjoyable workout. And... first puzzle find today: A Thursday from May 24, 2018 by Erik Agard and Andy Kravis. Some theme clues and answers: "Horse races?" WHINNYMEETS "Seinfeld's string instrument?" JERRYCELLO "Particularly pale Ph.D. ceremony?" PASTYHOODING And the reveal: "What 18-, 25-, 37- and 52-across all are: SPOONERISMS Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=5/24/2018&g=61&d=A Might put another puzzle find in a reply. ...

Bottom right corner was not fun at all. 5 proper names crossing each other, and two abbreviations.

The rest of the puzzle was beautiful! Really enjoyed the cluing
Absolute disrespect to label the realm of the super athlete that is Luke Littler as a 'game' rather than a 'sport'.

Well, as they said in yesterday's puzzle, OOF!  I felt pretty good about myself after breezing through NW, thanks to a pair of lucky guesses (BIRTHS and BOBSLED) right out of the gate. And then...nothing. Or nothing easy, anyway. Not that I did myself any favors entering "kush" instead of CASH and then "karma" instead of CABAL. But wow. Really, really impressed with this one, even if my Friday average just ticked upward. Thanks for the challenge!

Curious about how games are made? Interested in learning more about the editors behind them? Today at 1p.m. E.D.T., editor Tracy Bennett will be answering questions about all things Wordle. 

Head over to today’s Wordle Review to leave your question in the comment section ahead of a Q&A with Tracy.

Amazingly, I didn't find this too difficult! The lack of brand names, sport, colleges, and only one little rap name helped. 

It had to be STILTON, such an  English choice (not really Scots) and warm memories of my Dad who was in the RAF in WW2. He used to say 'raf' rather than R.A.F. and wore his 'raf' tie when seeing the bank manager or similar. 

I didn't know about the Red Delicious apple; American apples are reckoned to be large, shiny and tasteless in the UK ..... possibly a rumour put about by Cox's Orange Pippins and Worcester Pearmains. I wouldn't have got HENRI or JEFF except for the crossers.

I have DADO rails in my house, and I didn't know there was a secondary meaning. Good to learn.

The clues were the sort that I like - BIRTHS, DAFFODIL, GAME.
Excellent!

Though puzzle today.  Took me like 50 minutes, but all the clues were fun.  The kind of puzzle full of "Ahas".  Only a few niche proper nouns.

For the Martin Luther question I was thinking RELIGION for a while, but it didn't work with HEALS, took me a bit to finally think of THEOLOGY.

Some clever misdirects like DAFFODIL.

*dials 867-5309*

"Hee haw!"

Reading other people's comments, a lot of people had a lot of trouble with SE.  My hardest area was actually NE.

In SE got KARENINA and DRYAD immediately, then with DVD even though I'd never seen Welcome Back Cotter, the only name that fits V__N__ is VINNIE.  Then once I got PDFFILES the other tough proper nouns had enough letters to guess.

But in NE, since I originally didn't get the INLAW question and had INLAY, was looking for something ending in STRAY for THE LAST STRAW.  And even after I got THEOLOGY, ATHLETE and TEASPOON were tough to get.

Excellent puzzle! Perfect difficulty for a Friday.

Like many, I was hung up on the SE corner for the longest time. "Acrobat's display" was delightful once I figured out what was going on, though.
Excellent puzzle!  When you have TEAS_OON, you have to accept that the unknown rapper could actually be called ELP.  I’m glad the column explained that it’s pronounced EL-P.

I find the most clever and trickiest puzzles are those that seem to invite incorrect entries. Entries you are sure must be right, but which eventually prove to be wrong. 54a I was certain I was being brilliant by insisting it must be some form of ELEVENS_ or ELEVENIT, even though the tenses didn’t agree, because “taking it to 11” is definitely the most clever, inside version of “taking things up a notch.”  Similarly in 16a RELIGION had the right number of letters, but proved wrong. There were several that fit into this latter category. I often wonder if these seeming bait-and-switches are deliberate on the part of the constructor, or purely coincidental. Having solved for a number of years now, this is a phenomenon that comes up consistently with harder, well-crafted puzzles, so I like to assume they are intentional, and in which case, the cap is doffed, because that’s some dope a— sh—.
Shout out to D'Aulaire's Greek Mythology for making sure I will always know how to spell the various kinds of nymphs needed to get a Y or a few consecutive vowels in there.

Thank you! That was fun! This felt like a Friday, as well as very well engineered: I had a tough start of it, but once I got going, it had a way of guiding me through. Lots of nice clues throughout. More, please!

One of my favorite puzzles in a long while. I appreciated so many misdirects: 35D, “Acrobat’s display,” for PDF FILES; 49A, “It has a light bulb,” for DAFFODIL; 28A, “Peace sign?” for DO NOT DISTURB; 18A, “Low volume,” for TEASPOON….

Thought for a while I’d have to look up a couple but I managed to push through in about 10% under my average time.
Oh gosh I had dentist chair instead of desert island for about thirty minutes today.
A daffodil bulb is the only bulb I have ever seen. Is it unusually light coloured or light in weight?

My one SPARE AXON must have been firing today. It allowed this puzzle to SNEAKUP on me. 
I like how it's a party of names down there in the SE - JENNY, KAREN, HENRI, JEFF, VINNIE. 
The DAFFODILs are all out in force here, btw. So cheerful!
TGIF
Amazing construction and very clever clueing! Squandered way too much time assuming BACALL-MACARONI crosser in NW corner, but great fun with the rest of the puzzle. Well done, Mr. Spooner!
Fun and tricky crossword. I did waste 10 minutes because I had MACARONI in the top left instead of RIGATONI

?!?!?!
What the flip?!

Lots of fun misdirects. No Spoonerisms noted.
Thanks for this puzzle, Andrew.

This was haaaaaarrrrrdddd... and amazing! The SE's confluence of JEFF, HENRI, JENNY, and VINNIE was a tough pull, with JENNY fortunately providing a tenuous anchor for me. I think I only had two or three words placed after my first pass, but got there in the end. Really amazingly good clueing.

Nice one! Very slow to start, a real accelerando experience. Loved how many words fit--and were wrong.

More later. Gotta yoga now.
“Acrobat's display” was a misdirection I couldn't overcome. Bravo to the constructor. A worthy Friday puzzle.
What? No Spoonerisms?
155% slower than my average Friday per xwstats, and I still loved every second of it. Fun puzzle!

A very nice grid and some great clues.  My FAV (isn't this usually abbreviated FAVE?) was "You just can't take it" for the wonderful answer THE LAST STRAW.  I also like "Peace sign" for DO NOT DISTURB and the clues for DAFFODIL and INLAW.

OMG!  Another 3-letter singer to remember!  To ENO (whom we already have here), along with DRE and SIA, we must now add ELP!  HELP!!!

HEALS and LOSS kept me from writing in RELIGION where THEOLOGY goes.  But I did write in DESERTED ISLE before DESERT ISLAND -- straightened out by UNIFY.  I was careless here, not checking enough crosses first.  So my grid is messy.

To the OTTER clue I say Awww.  So sweet.  To the DADOS answer I say [46A].  *You know what I'm saying.  I must appease the bot algorithm.  Should we call it the botgorithm?)

Easy in the NW, this got harder as it went along.  I found it very enjoyable.
Whisky Tango Foxtrot?! That goes TOO FAR! No, actually I'm fine with it, as it's certainly in my vernacular.
Joining the chorus of masochists who loved the old-school challenge of this puzzle. More like this please! Took me 1/3 longer than average and couldn't be happier
Southeast corner was the trickiest, but I got it, after some thinking.
Always thinking, "I want a harder puzzle".  Then I get one.

Here I am....  in the PNW, and therefore comparatively  *Late to the Party*  ...well, it took me a bit to break into this puzzle, which was plainly full of misdirection, trickery, etc.
I am sure I'm not alone in entering IVANS at 4D.  Plus I have been  known to "get TEARY" at a wedding; haven't you?

My one Nit:  FAV instead of FAVE.  Now, I *know* that DAFFODIL was worth it, but still....

And then a semi-nit:  47D "Where the Red Delicious apple originated"--  the one, true four-letter answer is indisputably: HELL.

A few too many gimmes for a Friday, but this one still took me thirty minutes to finish, so it seemed alright for an end-of-the-week puzzle. 

Didn't know DADOS, but fortunately knew STILTON. Also, ELP meant nothing to me, but TEASPOON seemed right, though I haven't quite understood "Low volume"---unless it's simply a cutesy replacement for "small volume". The SE was a bit too laden with names---JEFF, HENRI, VINNIE, KARENINA---but I knew the last two and the others were gotten easily enough from the crosses.

Seemed like a decent Friday puzzle, all in all.

The otter clue warmed my heart because it reminded me of my cat Jasper, a ragdoll who is no longer with us but when he was, he slept between my wife and I.  I sleep on my side with my arm perpendicular to my chest, palm up, and Jasper would place his paw on my palm, and sleep with it there all night.  OMG that always felt so good.  Miss you, big boy.
Very good job Andrew. It was a real head scratcher until I finally caved and revealed 14D. After that, everything fell into place. Thanks for a formidable Friday.
Now that was a good puzzle—fun, challenging, and appropriate for Friday. 👍
Crunchy!

Wow! That was a perfect Friday—love one that seems impenetrable but slowly opens up.

Lots of fun stuff lately, but I’ve had to limit my typing due to a two-week-old shoulder surgery. The one answer that resonated (not in a good way) for me today was LIEAWAKE. Here’s to better sleep in a month or so…

Cheers, all!😊

Today's poem made from words found in today's puzzle

a/ for you might be a tree
you might be a tree
in a field washed in stars
and who’s to say you might
for the world surreptitiously be
the “neural transmitter” tree
d/ the one that thought that goes where thought goes
the thought you hold that every thought you
hold might hold you down
that one
that thought
that you
a/ at last awake unafraid
Anybody else get stuck mistaking THEOLOGY for the cloth?
Thank you, Karenina!! What a great Friday...bravo, Andrew!! Tolstoy himself would have loved it. Great word play/cluing.

My kids call me DADO. Maybe they think I'm groovy.

Good puzzle today!

A "denial" is a statement. 
It is not "no comment".

..... anddddd..... I'm done!!! A mere eleven hours later. 
Phew. Sweat pours from my brow, my typing fingers shake, I'm dehydrated and weak, I've neglected my friends and loved ones, I'm a pale shadow of the man I was when long ago I began solving this puzzle. 
It woulda gone faster but for some stupid reason I didn't memorize who was Grand Duke of Luxembourg until 2025 and now my regret is keen. 
I'm so weak right now. Send good wishes and a saline drip. 
PS. I think the term might be "deserted island," not "desert island." Meaning when one asks "What ten albums would you take with you ..." they mean to live on a deserted island, devoid of others; not on an arid one. But I quibble precisely because I just learned this tidbit a year ago myself. Back when ol' Henri was still Duke. 
The correct answer, by the way: Sgt. Pepper, Pet Sounds, Quadrophenia, Dark Side of the Moon, Blue, Zep 1, Legend, Astral Weeks, Rumours, CSN debut album.
Incredibly satisfying Friday puzzle, thank you!

What a great Friday puzzle!

And how adorable was that clue about holding paws while asleep? Those cute animals are always good to see in the crossword. Vinnie and I would both like to say “Welcome back, otter!”

(Did you know otters sometimes choose to sleep floating on their backs rather than return to land? If the water is rough, though, they may toss and turn, thinking as they lie awake: “Why a lake?”)

Holy cow.  That was a slog, and thank goodness I remembered “VINNIE”…that finally blew open the SE corner and got the solve.  

Damn good Friday (but not Good Friday).

One more puzzle find. Not sure how much of a chance I would have had with this one. A Sunday from December 25, 1994 by Randolph Ross with the title "Joy to the World." Some clues and answers: "Season's greetings from Athens." KALACHRISTOUGENNA "Season's greetings from Seoul" CHOOKSUNGTAN "Season's greetings from Oslo." GLEDELIGJUL "Season's greetings from Oahu." MELEKALIKIMAKA "Season's greetings from Dublin." NOLLAIGSHONADHUIT And a couple we might at least have had chances at: Rome - BUONNATELE Paris - JOYEUXNOEL Madrid - FELIZNAVIDAD Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=12/25/1994&g=74&d=A ....
This might be sleep deprivation talking, but that was the toughest Friday in recent memory. And I loved it!

Such fun!!

...I started out pondering all the 7-letter downhill sports (Bobsled? Slalom? Ski jump?) and that turned out to be the very least of my challenges -  what an excellent puzzle!
I was so disappointed that DRUNK wasn't something you might get at a wedding. It's the only thing I look forward to at weddings.
this was so tuff !

Well, I'll be! I honestly, 100%, thought the answer to a Red Delicious apple's origin was hell. Today I learned. 

Very fun, tough puzzle for me! Really enjoyed it! Went a bit slower than my norm, but it was all good! Many fun misdirects. Slow and steady won the race! Hip, hip, hurumba and huzzah!! 

After my first go through, pretty much the only (of the few) answers I was confident of were KARENINA crossing VINNIE, which really shows my range, I'd like to think. 

I haven't read any comments yet and I have to get back to being somewhat BUSINESSLIKE, but I hope people generally enjoyed this one!!

This one made me feel like I was solving with the upperclassmen at a top notch school.  No nonsense, long on thinking, short on esoterica and a blast of entertainment all the way.  I liked all of it. Beautiful cluing.

Thank you Andrew.
First pass was alarming! But, as usual, once I got started, the answers filled in. Nice puzzle and good cluing made for my slow start.
Love this one! Thought it was going to break my streak after the first pass.

Great, a Friday puzzle that put up a good fight! It took a lot of patience. I got it solved the same way I always solve tough puzzles: put it down, do something else, and come back later. 

For example, I took a break and when I returned I immediately realized 35D was PDF something.

Aside from that, I didn't and still don't like 49A, the light bulbs and daffodils one. It just doesn't sit right! And 1D, I guess BOBSLEDS do go downhill but they aren't out in the open air on a hill. No biggie.

HENRI? Henri the terrible, that one?
Absolute slog, contrived from the first clue to the last. A hard puzzle without crossed obscure nouns or just straight up misdirection, is that so much to ask?

This required a lot of patience and a nap in between, but it was worth it in the end. Today was where you fill in something and see where it goes, and then backtrack if it is not going anywhere. And use some Sudoku like logic to see which letters cannot logically fit in the squares ahead according to English phonology. Quite fun, although I could have done with a fewer proper nouns in the bottom right section.

Loved this. Was staring at a frightening amount of blank space after my first few passes, but slowly things started filling up and then suddenly this felt very solvable. Great clues and brilliant misdirects. I'd say the juice was worth the squeeze. Well done!
This was so absoLUTELY worth the 53ish minutes it took to get there! https://www.youtube.com/shorts/ZOj-3W8GGAk Enjoyed every circuitous minute of it

N16nN6mq7ygy7goq
Going
B
Yh6gkk

Fun puzzle today! I love Fridays. I have one issue today with the clue for Brian Eno (25 A). I would never call him a rocker. Musician yes, rocker no. Influential in modern rock/ ambient music, his name is often used in these crosswords. https://mixdownmag.com.au/features/how-brian-eno-influenced-music-as-we-know-it-today/
Why can't I access the Midi puzzle consistency,  Sometimes, it does and sometimes, it does not. I can be in the middle of it and close it and go back in and it is no longer available on NYT.

Encore!  Encore!  It reminded me of the level of difficulty in the 80s and 90s.  Acrobat's display, Peace sign, Low volume.  That is some outstanding and enjoyable wordplay sir.   I haven't had so much aggravation, frustration, revelation and fun doing a crossword in years.  I hope to be enjoying more of your works soon, Mr. Spooner.  Thank you.
Is the creator of today's puzzle a Brit? Because certain answers make more sense that way.

Wow That was hard! At first I thought I couldn’t do it at all but read some encouraging comments and found ENO was in fact the answer to “Rocker Bryan”. Some of my early guesses were right but not all: For “Green spots” I had Fives for OASES.

At minute 35’ I was stuck in the SW and lower middle center, checked a couple of words and then was able to finish but didn’t read Wordplay Hints below the fold:

I had Rejects for RESALES and no idea what ESSAY was for “Expo” because I thought World. 
So yes some great misdirects or call it ambiguity if you will.
PDF files was ridiculous enough without crossing equally ridiculous clues. The entire SW corner destroyed an already slog of a puzzle
Good.  Creative. Challenging.  Fair.  More Fridays and Saturdays like this please.
Rocker to Brian Eno is like slugger to Phil Rizzutto.
If you’ve never had oxtail soup I feel sad for you. We used to make it all the time but the price of them is about that of strip steaks now. Oh right, fun challenging puzzle too!
Wow, this one was awesome. SE corner was killing me until "Acrobat's display" finally clicked. Finished in 24:33

I hated this puzzle. Way too many vague one word clues with crosses that didn’t help. 

Challenging? Yes, but painfully so. 

Rating:
1/10

I think the votes are in.

More Spoonerisms, please!
Sneaky, sneaky! And here I was swinging from a trapeze only to find out that everyone else was on a computer! LOL Good puzzle!

Finally,  a straightforward puzzle without gimmicks.

Funny that one the answers in today's Puzzle is apparently banned for use in this forum.

Using it when commenting on a puzzle earlier this week stopped my comment from being approved.  Pointing it out earlier today stopped my comment from being published.  

I'd love to hear Barry's comments on that subject.

(Hopefully this one isn't banned.)

Great puzzle, said even though I had to bail on the SW so DNF.   Great cluing, humorous misdirects;   Fun fill. 
And now to bed.   Peace sign .
Tough but fun (once it was done!)
I'm two years in, love a challenge, and this was just no fun. This was built for the elite. I am sure they enjoyed it and happy for them.
Can anyone explain “UNAFRAID”? Why is that a game?

Enjoyed this challenging Friday puzzle. Thanks, Andrew! 

One question I must ask: Shouldn't the clue for 1-Down be "Go (not "Goes") downhill at the Olympics, in a way," since the answer is plural? You wouldn't say "bobsleds goes downhill". . . unless you enjoy torturing English majors for some reason? ;)

This is the kind of puzzle I really enjoy—had to work hard, each time I picked it up I got a few more, and I was determined as I have a streak started. When I got it, music to my ears! Last to fall was unafraid. Very clever clues very few proper names (I confess my husband gave me Vinnie, used to love that show and its song).
Phenomenal! I really enjoyed this one.

@Jake G Pretty much my experience exactly with this one too. I was surprised to finish under my Friday average since I never felt like anything was easy during the solve, but had to just steadily beat it into submission. I love a puzzle that keeps fighting until the bitter end!

@Alex Sadu! Well said! 

This really could engender a different direction for the conversation that goes on and on on this board about the difficulty of puzzles. I’ve been complaining less lately because I see that I’m gradually becoming more intuitive when it comes to them. Also, an answer I might have seen only once or twice in the last couple of years comes back to me. 

Yes, the Thursday, Friday and Saturday puzzles might be getting a little easier. And, maybe, we’re all just getting better. I really think today’s puzzle would have taken me over an hour four or five years ago if I would have gotten it at all without any assistance.

It’s so weird in a lifetime that’s finishing its seventh decade that one of the great thrills of my life is completing a puzzle like this when I was sitting there clueless at about the 50% mark.

Thanks for your reply Alex.
@Alex These two comments encouraged me to keep going and well start!
@Dan I'm with you! Took me 36 minutes, 12 longer than an average Friday. But I enjoyed it the entire time!
@Oaktowner Wow, our times are very similar! Took me 35.5 minutes, 12 longer than average.

@Dan
Amen!  40 minutes for me, but it was the kind of test I enjoy the most. Every answer was completely fair, but many took some working out, 35D being the last.
In fact, I saw Jack McCoy saying that exact phrase yesterday, which only confirms it.

@John

Emo started out with Roxy Music, and his first couple of solo albums were rock also, so the clue is fair, but yes his real fame is in the field of ambient music.

@John
My favorite clue for him is something along the lines of “creator of Windows 95 startup sound”. 
Three cells. 
You know it’s either ANI difranco, yoko ONO, or our boy.

@John
Check out ‘Third Uncle’ from Eno’s album ‘Taking Tiger Mountain (by Strategy)’

Hard driving frenetic rock.  
It was the first Eno piece I heard and it was a shock for me to learn of his ambient work.  

One eclectic artist!

@Marshall Walthew

Plus ENO shows up like twice a week minimum so anyone who regularly does crosswords the moment they see "Brian" in the clue should be thinking it.

@John I've been a fan of Eno since high school, and he really has been all over the map. check out the Wikipedia article on him. In addition to Roxy Music (he/they were inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame in 2019) and his solo work, he has done seminal work with David Bowie, and famously produced Talking Heads, Devo, U2, Coldplay, Peter Gabriel, Grace Jones and several other bands and artists. Funny that @Kevin Sparks mentioned "Third Uncle"; a different song, from Before and After Science, has been an earworm for me for the past several days. Looking for a recording to post here is sending me down a rabbit hole of songs I haven't heard in several years. Here's "Backwater". Some really intricate lyrics in there: But if you study the logistics And heuristics of the mystics You will find that their minds rarely move in a line So it's much more realistic To abandon such ballistics And resign to be trapped on a leaf in the vine here's a link: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=RvciU4AJ5Cc

@Kevin Sparks
thanks for the rabbit hole. ;)

@SP

I had never noticed before that THEOLOGY and religion have the same number of letters.

@Steve L
I started to fill in "religion," then decided to wait a bit until THEOLOGY almost filled itself in.

@Steve L

As does MINISTRY
@Emma apologies, misread your constructor note — I will have to look for your older puzzle(s) :)

@Emma
Agree! 25% faster than my Friday average.

@Emma

I solved this some 20% faster than average BUT it was super hard. I think solve time works as a measure of difficulty only to some extent. A very hard but well thought-out puzzle often solves more quickly for me than a less-well constructed one. Here I struggled until I didn't - everything finally fell into place perfectly.

@Emma

“This is why I think we should pay less attention to the complaints in this forum about declining difficulty because there will always be different strokes for different folks”

I’m definitely not going to argue that you should pay *more* attention to any comments in this forum, but I will ask: have you spent much time solving puzzles from 10+ years ago in the archives?

It’s absolutely true that solvers’ perceptions of difficulty can diverge wildly for any one puzzle, but the complaints about declining difficulty don’t hinge upon just one or two (or even ten or twenty) puzzles…

@Paul There is something very strange about a newspaper fearing a word billions use. We all know it, most of us use it, but noooo, a serious newspaper can't publish it! Can't it though? https://www.theguardian.com/media/2015/nov/07/guardian-f-word-alastair-hetherington-lady-chatterley

@Andrzej

In that article, I find the words “trump cards” much more offensive.
@Steve L I think people hear SNAFU as a word rather than an initialism, and many probably don't even know what the letters mean.

@Steve L
What a great political climate, whereby certain words and phrases have become cringe-worthy. The term “trump” has definitely become a paramount trigger word. I find myself avoiding using the word anymore and thankfully, auto check doesn't capitalize it. 😋

Additionally, phrases such as “to be clear,” “the science,” and “yet, here we are” have gained ground. While I used to be offended by the terms “snowflake” and “acorn,” I no longer hold those sentiments.

I assume you're not a bridge player, but in the event you decide to start, these bridge terms simply cannot be avoided: 😋

- What's trump again?
- I bid 2 no trump
- Get your trumps out there
- I knew she has the last trump
- We're not playing trump
- There is no trump

@Jerry

You can easily avoid using those terms. 

1. You could avoid playing bridge.

Or

2.  You can substitute another word.  Grump, bump, tromp, whatever. I’m partial to yutz, putz or schmuck.

@Andrzej
I think how much the f-word gets used may be regional. I'd never even heard the word until I was an adult, but then in a different milieu, heard it all the time, several times in every sentence, having replaced every other adjective in that particular vernacular. I did notice that people with a history in the military tended to use it liberally. 

It's not considered acceptable in many classrooms, so some parents don't want their kids to hear it until they are old enough to use some discretion.

Which can be problematic for me if I stub my toe. 😅

@Bob also many people learn it as "Situation Normal All Fouled Up" which seems reasonable until you're told otherwise. + @Steve L I remember Stephen Colbert having one of the cast members of Schitt's Creek on the show, and he mentioned that yes they were allowed to say the name of the show, as long as every time they did they flashed the logo of the title with the spelling of Schitt. On a *total* tangent, have you ever seen the K-mart "Ship My Pants" commercial? I can't believe they got away with this: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=I03UmJbK0lA

@Lynn

Poles swear *a lot* but there is so much hypocrisy around it! Almost everybody says their kurwas daily, but when somebody does so publicly, people are oh so outraged. It's just siIIy. My professor, author and public servant mother, one of my heroes, cussed like a sailor, and I take after her in that 🤣. I can speak and write perfect, literary Polish, but rolling the R if a juicy kurwa feels great!

@Andrzej
My dad the minister never swore until I turned 18. Then he took me aside and gave me a half hour swearing lesson. He said if I was going to do it, I was going to do it right.

From that day onward, he would use everything he learned in the Navy if my younger siblings weren't around. 

He also stopped eating his vegetables when we turned 18. Turns out he'd been eating them all those years purely as a role model.

@Jess
i'm reading comments late and in order; i wonder what xwstats has to say? I have yet to sign up.
@UCCF Exactly the same for me.
@UCCF Same; I’m not sure why it unfolded so slowly for me today, and I nearly lost hope when I filled in nothing but “ENO” on my first pass.

@Andrzej

Thanks for the Lucek photo yesterday.  That is a charming picture.
@Andrzej Same.

SE corner was brutal.

@Bill in Yokohama @Andrzej
The only gimme for me was JENNY, with KARENINA and DVD as guesses that proved correct. RAF was slow to fall, despite having heard Ad Astra Per Aspera on Star Trek: Strange New Worlds....
OMG!! looking for a link I discovered that that clue is a mistake!!
must go create a new post.

@Bob T.
And, as usual, false alarm. The clue is good. My mistake: the phrase on ST:SNW was "Ad Astra per Aspera", which is among other things the official state motto of Kansas. The clue doesn't use the actual latin phrase, and the RAF's motto is " Per ardua ad astra"--slightly different but same rough translation. I humbly take the L and dine on crow.

@ALH 
Yes! I actually filled in MAY at first. No disrespect to Brian Eno, he's pretty awesome, but while he is an accomplished musician I think of him more as a producer.

@NanuNanu exactly the same here, got stuck in a "Backwater" for a while and had to wander "through hollow lands" until "everything merges in the night" and it all came together. It was definitely "golden hours" when that happened.

His solo albums from the mid-70s are a fantastic fusion of pop & prog rock. Always worth a listen.

@Francis

Are you being phiolosophical?

I mean, what's so important about *anything*?

Personally though, food is one of the greatest joys of my life, and if anything is important to me, at all, food is it.

However, I've never had stilton.

@Weak

commentBody:
@Weak

Recent "ROQUEFORT" as an Answer
The full 9-letter word is a relatively rare answer because of its length, but it has appeared on these dates:
October 3, 2021 (Sunday)
October 1, 2017 (Sunday)
August 10, 2011 (Wednesday)
January 31, 2010 (Sunday)

Google AI says "Neener neener."

@Francis Speaking as a Brit leaving in France, i can offer a few thoughts:

1) Stilton is known (by Brits) as “the King of Cheese

2) i strongly suspect this is an attempt to pretend that British cheese is better than French cheese (cheese being one of the few areas where the uppity French might try to assert their primacy)

3) i see an excellent comment about Stilton on Reddit, that “it tastes like sick but in a good way”

4) Stilton is probably easier to eat if you are a flag-waving (ie slightly loony and possibly certifiably mad) British patriot and you have to believe Britain is better than France in all things

5) there is loads of excellent British cheese, especially hard cheese, but the French are the global champions, especially for soft cheese and blue cheese

What happened there...

My dead mother*

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
@Andrzej

There's a joke percolating in my melon about "Polish" and "intelligence" and "AI" but it'd probably get emu'ed anyway...

"dead is my wife" and "my dead mother"....hmmm.

Is there a psychoanalyst on the plane?...STAT!
@Francis I took it as a tautological quip in the vein of “if you like that sort of thing it’s just the sort of thing you’ll like” but maybe I was overthinking it

@Francis
In my humble experience STILTON cheese has an almost mythical status in the UK. It’s the only cheese for aristocratic tables, paired with Port (ALWAYS pass to the left, no, I have no idea why either). Like Marmite you either love it or hate it. I love it, but it has to be room temp. Fridge cold STILTON is an abomination, but a mistake that many people make, thereby thinking they dislike the delicacy.

@Francis

I think your Einstein quote would make a GREAT clue...if only he had said it!

@Francis

I thought that was a clever clue, and, of the many tricky clues, I thought it was one of the easier ones.

Before I had any crosses I slapped down CHEDDAR, which is a cheese the British can rightfully be proud of.
But STILTON?  It can't hold a candle to a decent ROQUEFORT or GORGONZOLA or even good ol' MAYTAG BLUE.
@Petrol Uh… have you heard of Wisconsin? We may not approach France in overall breadth; however, we make some pretty good cheese. 😊

@Jake G
I remember driving across the so-called "dairy state" many years ago. Stopped a a roadside cafe....had to ask for butter instead of the margarine offered, and even had to ask for real milk/cream instead of the non-dairy horror they expected me to spoil my coffee with. Hopefully, they've upped their game since then! Just sayin'.

@Matt

I don't know how the AI slop was researched, but here's the correct info, from xwordinfo.com:

5 total results for ROQUEFORT( score: 50 )
3 results for ROQUEFORT from Variety puzzles:
Date	Type	Grid	Clue	Author	Editor

Aug 27, 2000	Acrostic	V	Sort of cheese that is ripened in caves	Emily Cox & Henry Rathvon	Shortz

Sun Apr 29, 1951	60A	Cheese, ripened in caves.	E. T. Webster	Farrar

Sorry for the poor formatting.

@Andrzej
Although I'm kind of a cheese snob, I must say my favorite in all the world is some White Stilton with Cranberry my local food coop imports from England every Christmas.

Francis — I love Stilton cheese, but this cheese is often misunderstood. When young it could be easily confused with a blue cheese. But if the Stilton is allowed to age, then at the point of ripeness is has a wonderful walnutty arugula-like flavor that is out of this world.

@Lynn

Your post made me wonder if I had a favorite cheese, or even favorite food. I think I don't, actually. I enjoy good food, and there still is so much out there I haven't tried! Of course there are things I love from childhood, but even though they evoke memories and feelings, it's not that they are the best things I have ever eaten.
@Petrol Ahem, Wisconsin would like a word...

@Matt I actually didn't know it, so I got quite a kick out of looking it up and bookmarking the letter. if anyone else is interested: https://www.orwellfoundation.com/the-orwell-foundation/orwell/essays-and-other-works/in-defence-of-english-cooking/ but be careful; when it comes to cheeses, some are more equal than others. And Big Brother is watching.

@Weak I didn't think we do say "desert island"--I've not done a search, but I was sure it's a "deserted island."

@Weak

Great question!
Here is the Google AI answer:

 "Desert island" uses an archaic form of "desert," meaning deserted, desolate, or uninhabited, rather than referring to a sandy, dry climate. While "deserted" means abandoned, "desert" implies it was never inhabited at all, though both now generally describe an isolated island with no people.

@George Krompacky

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
@Andrzej

Looked it up only to discover I'd already done it during a prior archive dive.

It was hard.

But when I reviewed it I re-experienced the bad-not-good butt-kicking sensations which I have learned to associate with this setter.

No shade.  He's his own kind of clever.

We just don't vibe.

@Andrzej
oy. I had done it (I'm going backwards and I'm in 2016) but looking at it i couldn't make sense of the theme answers today; i must have gotten them from crosses. I had to go re-read Wordplay [hey Deb Amlen! miss you] to make sense of it.

@Petrol

I really hope you're making that stuff up. I'll not fact-check so I can keep living the dream!

@Teresa

I studied Japanese for one semester. Particles attached to the ends of words are very common, perform many functions and are central to the construction of Japanese sentences. For example, adding the phoneme "ka" to the end of a sentence makes it into a question.

@lucky13

Adding the phoneme "do" to the beginning of a sentence in English makes it into a question.

@lucky13

(Actually, "do" is two or three phonemes, and I suspect so is "ka.")

@Steve L

You're right. I should have said "syllable" instead of "phoneme."

@Sonja

Somebody skated this season to a song of his (not Queen's) in one of the international competitions. I can't remember the title, but it was some of the worst musical dross I have ever heard in my life 😂

Even my wife - who is much more tolerant to bad music than I am - said her ears were bleeding 😢

So there!
@Sonja i decided that "rocker" in the clue was gently nodding me away from Eno so I put in May instead, took me a bit to unwind that mistake!

@Sonja
Exactly!! Brian is a rock guitarist (who I had the privilege to meet at a taxi rank in Edinburgh once and I gallantly gave him my cab)… eno is a pioneer but not a ‘rocker’…

@Sonja
He's more of a "rocker" than ENO, isn't he? I left those squares blank for a very long time in the hope that today was May's day. Alas.
@Gareth Brian Eno was with Roxy Music in the early 70s and did some great hard rock solo work after he left them. Listen to his song Third Uncle.
@Sonja I had May in for a while which made me think 1D was SKIJUMPS

@Sonja
Yes! I confidently put in May (before remembering this was the NYT). I also had Ivans instead of TSARS so that north west section took a while.

@Andrzej old pal
I went searching and couldn't find any non-Queen Brian May songs being used in international skating competition.  I did find this (and I hate Google's AI):

"Chinese skater Boyang Jin selected "Fought & Lost" by Sam Ryder featuring Brian May for his short program in the 2025/2026 season."

FWIW I have never heard anything from Sir Dr. Brian other than his work with Queen, but I've always been impressed by him. And I've never heard of Sam Ryder. :\

@Bob T.

That was it! A truly horrible song, and even if Mr May did not compose it, he must have liked it since he sang in it, no? That alone is a scary thought.
@Ray Errors are by no means rare here.

Ray, Wikipedia suggests the clue is correct: Ivan IV Vasilyevich (Russian: Иван IV Васильевич;[a] 25 August [O.S. 15 August] 1530 – 28 March [O.S. 18 March] 1584), commonly known as Ivan the Terrible,[b] was Grand Prince of Moscow and all Russia from 1533 to 1547, and the first Tsar and Grand Prince of all Russia from 1547 until his death in 1584. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ivan_the_Terrible

N.B. Indeed, there are clue and and answer errors in the crossword from time to time (and a recently misplaced grid). The actual errors are several orders of magnitude rarer than claims of errors in the comments.

@Barry Ancona

I second that comment.  

I also have a soft spot for meteorologists.  They're always getting slammed for bad forecasts, but IMO their right vastly more often than they're wrong, especially on temperature predictions.  Precipitation is more difficult, but I think they do a very good job at an exceedingly difficult task.

And rarely get any credit.

@Francis
The meteorologists on our local stations are so doggone good-looking, they have my full support, regardless of their accuracy. But, it is nice to know when to *not* wash my car or water my lawn. Both actions being a sure-fire way to invoke the rain Gods.

@lucky13

Oh, I forgot to mention that the death of an important person in Final Jeopardy, of course, referred to President Franklin Roosevelt who hid his disability from polio from most of the world most of the time. He usually appeared seated but if you see him standing in photos, he is always supported by another person by his side.

@lucky13
SRSLY, penicillin? Clearly he did not get the reference to FDR, who had suffered from polio.

@lucky13 - This was one of my "screaming at the screen" Jeopardy! episodes. I knew the final right away -- it was bloody obvious -- and assume everyone would know it, too. 

I knew Jamie would get it so I knew he would win, unless he didn't bet large. I  ASSumed the guy on the right would  know would know it, too. Nope!

It was good to see Jamie have to sweat a bit. A part of me wants to see him finally lose, but the other part wants to see if he can break all records, including Ken's. This is the first contestant in a long time who I think might do that. 

He working hard to improve the public image of "bureaucrats" everywhere.
Definitely a proper Friday. It seems many people found it a bit harder than recent Friday fare: https://xwstats.com/puzzles/2026-04-24

@Barry Ancona
thx for the link. I was wondering what they thought of it over there.

@97Bravo
no shame in that. I'm a longtime solver and I wondered if I was going to have to do a lookup. I didn't [I don't anymore except for old archive puzzles], but the grid was slow to yield. I came in just barely longer than my average, and I usually beat it these days, so xwstats' designation  of "very hard" seems apt.

@The X-Phile
I don't time myself, but it seemed that I solved it a bit faster than my typical Friday time. The SE corner was the last to fall, but luckily I remembered JENNY.
@Steve L 16 minute median solve time? Who are you people? Geez! An hour and three for me…. Sorry to skew the stats in the wrong direction.

@Spenser
People who are willing to pay for a site to track more stats and look up old clues. Not your average nyt games subscriber.

I solved this in 21 minutes, and my Friday average is some 25 minutes. Each of us has their own pace I guess. As Niall says, only the most avid cruciverbalists sign up for Xwordinfo, and they are the fastest among us.

@Niall

There’s no charge for this site. It has nothing to do with xwordinfo, which is a pay site and provides the database you’re thinking of. 

When you sign up for xwstats, you have to sync your stats at the NYT site with their site. It’s a little tricky but is not necessarily easier to do just because you are an expert solver. 

Remember that on a golf course, a par-three hole is not one everyone can expect to get in three shots. But it’s a useful guide to how difficult the hole is anyway.

@Andrzej

Again, Niall is writing about xwordinfo, but the stats come from xwstats.com, which is a different site.

@Helen Wright

My wife and I traveled all over Jamaica in 2011 but I can't remember oxtail soup featuring on menus. If only I knew if it was there but I just can't recall it, or maybe it's not popular even if it once was. I liked my memory better when I was younger!

@Helen Wright
The thought of eating oxtail anything would make me gag. But a few weeks ago, a friend said she took out oxtail soup from a local restaurant. I got curious, so I did some research. And that's when I learned that it's actually a cow's tail. But then I learned about the gelatinous part, and I gagged again. I can't even tolerate gelatinous soups from Chinese restaurants. I'll stick with chicken noodle or mulligatawny.

@Times Rita
I’ll admit it is an acquired taste. But honestly there’s nothing nicer on a cold, dark winter’s day than oxtail stew that’s been cooking for 10-12 hours. The meat is rich, mouth-wateringly tender and flavoursome. The bone marrow is so deliciously soft you can spread it on toast. A massive hit of all the B vitamins and iron in one delicious dish.

@Times Rita
it doesn't taste gelatinous; the gelatin dissolves into the soup or stew making it terrifically rich. That's pretty standard when cooking with bones/marrow/gelatin--they have to be slow-cooked, but it's pretty rewarding. My eldest brother contributes oxtail soup to Thanksgiving dinner most years. Oddly, my caribbean-ancestry brother-in-law avoids it--the word alone bugs him. go figure!
I have to add that I adored [Accounted for] for HERE, [It has a light bulb] for DAFFODIL, and the superbly vague [Game] for UNAFRAID.

@Jane Wheelaghan
During my childhood in the 1960s, the Red Delicious apple was a favored choice. With the advent of the “factory culture” of everything in America, especially food, many farm fresh items have become banal. Fresh strawberries used to be soft, succulent, juicy and red on the inside. Now, my God, they’re like tiny apples with a hint of strawberry flavor!

A former work colleague of mine from (forgot the African nation) once commented on how our avocados lack any taste. All US avocados are imported from Mexico. There is an actual cartel that controls avocado exports from Mexico (imagine that, in America). He hated them, since the avocados from his country are delicious and tickle the tastebuds like no other.

Don’t get me started on our factory-fresh tomatoes. They’re apples under the guise of tomatoes! The bananas are still mostly palatable, as are the celery and iceberg lettuce.

I really miss the days of actual fresh fruit and vegetables, but I’m old and irreverent, and who cares about Boomers.

Send me some Worcester Pearmains, PLEASE!
